title: Warfarin ↔ vitamin K
detail: Vitamin K opposes warfarin's anticoagulant effect and can shift INR unpredictably.
instruction: Keep vitamin K intake consistent; do not start or stop K supplements without prescriber guidance and INR monitoring.

title: Warfarin ↔ high-dose omega-3
detail: High-dose omega-3 fatty acids may add to antiplatelet effects and increase bleeding risk with warfarin.
instruction: Discuss fish oil dose with your clinician; report bruising or bleeding; INR may need monitoring.

title: Warfarin ↔ vitamin E (>400 IU/day)
detail: Vitamin E at higher doses may increase bleeding risk when combined with warfarin.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin E unless your care team approves; monitor for bruising or bleeding.

title: Warfarin ↔ CoQ10
detail: Some reports suggest CoQ10 may reduce warfarin effect but evidence is inconsistent and weak.
instruction: If you use CoQ10, inform your prescriber. INR monitoring when starting or stopping is prudent.
source: MedlinePlus / FDA label interactions — evidence mixed

title: Warfarin ↔ garlic supplement
detail: Garlic supplements may increase bleeding risk with anticoagulants.
instruction: Discuss garlic supplements with your pharmacist; watch for unusual bruising or bleeding.

title: Warfarin ↔ ginkgo biloba
detail: Ginkgo may inhibit platelet function and increase bleeding risk with warfarin.
instruction: Avoid combining unless your clinician directs otherwise; report bleeding promptly.

title: Warfarin ↔ turmeric/curcumin
detail: Turmeric/curcumin may have antiplatelet activity and affect bleeding risk with warfarin.
instruction: Discuss turmeric supplements with your care team; monitor INR if you start or stop.

title: Warfarin ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort induces drug-metabolizing enzymes and can markedly lower warfarin levels.
instruction: Avoid St. John's Wort with warfarin unless explicitly approved with INR monitoring.

title: Warfarin ↔ high-dose vitamin C
detail: Very high doses of vitamin C have been associated with altered warfarin response in some reports.
instruction: Discuss megadose vitamin C with your prescriber; maintain consistent intake if approved.

title: Apixaban ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 supplements may add to bleeding risk with apixaban.
instruction: Discuss dose with your clinician; seek care for major bleeding or prolonged bruising.

title: Apixaban ↔ vitamin E
detail: Higher-dose vitamin E may increase bleeding risk with anticoagulants including apixaban.
instruction: Use vitamin E doses only as directed by your care team; report bleeding symptoms.

title: Rivaroxaban ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 may increase bleeding risk when taken with rivaroxaban.
instruction: Discuss fish oil with your prescriber; report signs of bleeding.

title: Dabigatran ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 may add to bleeding risk with dabigatran.
instruction: Discuss supplement use with your clinician before starting high-dose fish oil.

title: Statin ↔ CoQ10
detail: Statins lower CoQ10 synthesis; supplementation is commonly discussed for muscle symptoms.
instruction: Discuss whether CoQ10 fits your plan; report unexplained muscle pain to your clinician.

title: Simvastatin/lovastatin ↔ grapefruit
detail: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4 and can raise blood levels of simvastatin and lovastatin, increasing myopathy risk.
instruction: Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice with these statins unless your prescriber advises otherwise.

title: Statin ↔ high-dose niacin
detail: Combined statin and high-dose niacin may increase risk of muscle injury (myopathy/rhabdomyolysis).
instruction: Do not add high-dose niacin to a statin without medical supervision and monitoring.

title: Statin ↔ red yeast rice
detail: Red yeast rice contains monacolin K (a lovastatin-like compound) and can double statin exposure.
instruction: Do not combine red yeast rice with prescription statins unless directed by your prescriber.
source: FDA / NIH NCCIH red yeast rice

title: Levothyroxine ↔ calcium
detail: Calcium binds levothyroxine in the gut and reduces absorption.
instruction: Separate levothyroxine and calcium by at least 4 hours.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ iron
detail: Iron reduces levothyroxine absorption when taken together.
instruction: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from iron supplements.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium can reduce levothyroxine absorption.
instruction: Separate doses by at least 4 hours.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ zinc
detail: Zinc may reduce levothyroxine absorption.
instruction: Take levothyroxine at least 4 hours apart from zinc.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ fiber/psyllium
detail: Fiber supplements can reduce levothyroxine absorption.
instruction: Separate by at least 2 hours from levothyroxine.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ soy
detail: Soy products may reduce levothyroxine absorption in some patients.
instruction: Keep soy intake consistent and separate large soy doses from thyroid medication when possible.

title: Levothyroxine ↔ coffee
detail: Coffee taken with levothyroxine can reduce absorption.
instruction: Take levothyroxine with water; wait 30–60 minutes before coffee.
source: Clinical studies cited in thyroid drug labeling

title: Levothyroxine ↔ high-dose biotin
detail: High-dose biotin can interfere with some thyroid blood tests (false results).
instruction: Tell your lab and clinician if you take biotin; stop high-dose biotin before labs if directed.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: Combining serotonergic antidepressants with St. John's Wort increases serotonin syndrome risk.
instruction: Do not combine without medical supervision; seek care for agitation, fever, or rapid heartbeat.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ 5-HTP
detail: 5-HTP adds serotonergic activity and can increase serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs/SNRIs.
instruction: Avoid combining unless explicitly directed by a psychiatrist or prescriber.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ SAMe
detail: SAMe may increase serotonergic activity when combined with antidepressants.
instruction: Discuss SAMe with your prescriber before use; report anxiety, tremor, or insomnia.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ tryptophan
detail: Tryptophan increases serotonin precursor load and may raise serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs.
instruction: Avoid tryptophan supplements with SSRIs unless your clinician approves.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 fatty acids are often studied as adjuncts to antidepressants with generally favorable safety profiles.
instruction: Continue prescribed antidepressants; discuss omega-3 dose with your care team.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ melatonin
detail: Melatonin may add to sedation or sleepiness with some antidepressants.
instruction: Use lowest effective melatonin dose; avoid driving if overly sedated.

title: Metformin ↔ vitamin B12
detail: Long-term metformin use is associated with lower vitamin B12 levels.
instruction: Discuss B12 monitoring or supplementation with your clinician.

title: Metformin ↔ chromium
detail: Chromium may affect glucose; combined use can increase hypoglycemia risk.
instruction: Monitor blood sugar if adding chromium; report dizziness or sweating.

title: Metformin ↔ berberine
detail: Berberine and metformin both lower blood glucose and may have additive effects.
instruction: Do not combine without medical supervision; monitor glucose closely.

title: Metformin ↔ alpha-lipoic acid
detail: Alpha-lipoic acid may lower blood glucose and add to metformin's effect.
instruction: Monitor blood sugar when starting ALA; discuss dose with your clinician.

title: Diabetes medication ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium is involved in glucose metabolism; supplementation may affect insulin sensitivity.
instruction: Monitor glucose if you add magnesium; keep your diabetes team informed.

title: Sulfonylurea ↔ chromium
detail: Chromium may enhance insulin action and increase hypoglycemia risk with sulfonylureas.
instruction: Monitor blood sugar closely if using chromium; know hypoglycemia symptoms.

title: ACE inhibitor ↔ potassium
detail: ACE inhibitors reduce potassium loss and supplementation can cause hyperkalemia.
instruction: Avoid potassium supplements or salt substitutes unless prescribed; check potassium labs.

title: ACE inhibitor ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium may add to blood pressure lowering with ACE inhibitors.
instruction: Monitor blood pressure when starting magnesium; report dizziness.

title: Beta blocker ↔ CoQ10
detail: CoQ10 is sometimes used alongside heart medications; evidence for harm with beta blockers is limited.
instruction: Discuss supplements with your cardiologist; do not stop beta blockers abruptly.

title: Calcium channel blocker ↔ grapefruit
detail: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal metabolism and can raise levels of many calcium channel blockers.
instruction: Avoid grapefruit with felodipine/nifedipine-like agents unless your prescriber approves.

title: CCB ↔ calcium supplements
detail: High calcium intake does not replace CCB therapy and very high doses may affect mineral balance.
instruction: Take calcium as directed; do not use calcium to replace blood pressure medication.

title: Blood pressure medication ↔ licorice root
detail: Licorice can raise blood pressure and oppose antihypertensive therapy.
instruction: Avoid licorice supplements with hypertension unless your clinician approves.

title: Blood pressure medication ↔ high-dose caffeine
detail: High caffeine intake can transiently raise blood pressure and blunt medication control.
instruction: Limit large caffeine doses; monitor home blood pressure.

title: PPI ↔ iron
detail: PPIs reduce stomach acid needed for optimal iron absorption.
instruction: Separate iron from PPI when possible; discuss labs if on long-term PPI.

title: PPI ↔ magnesium
detail: Long-term PPI use is associated with hypomagnesemia in some patients.
instruction: Discuss magnesium monitoring with your clinician if on PPIs long term.

title: PPI ↔ vitamin B12
detail: Reduced acid can impair B12 absorption over time with PPI therapy.
instruction: Consider B12 status monitoring with long-term PPI use.

title: PPI ↔ calcium carbonate
detail: Calcium carbonate needs acid for absorption; PPIs may reduce its effectiveness.
instruction: Consider calcium citrate with low acid states; separate timing from PPI if advised.

title: PPI ↔ zinc
detail: Low stomach acid may reduce zinc absorption during long-term acid suppression.
instruction: Discuss zinc needs with your clinician if on chronic PPI therapy.

title: Fluoroquinolone ↔ calcium
detail: Calcium chelates fluoroquinolones and reduces antibiotic absorption.
instruction: Separate fluoroquinolone and calcium by at least 2 hours.

title: Fluoroquinolone ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium reduces fluoroquinolone absorption.
instruction: Take antibiotic 2 hours before or 6 hours after magnesium.

title: Fluoroquinolone ↔ iron
detail: Iron binds fluoroquinolones and reduces absorption.
instruction: Separate doses by at least 2 hours.

title: Fluoroquinolone ↔ zinc
detail: Zinc can reduce fluoroquinolone absorption.
instruction: Separate zinc and fluoroquinolone by several hours.

title: Tetracycline ↔ calcium
detail: Calcium and dairy reduce tetracycline absorption.
instruction: Avoid calcium/dairy within 2 hours of tetracycline doses.

title: Tetracycline ↔ iron
detail: Iron reduces tetracycline absorption.
instruction: Separate iron and tetracycline by at least 2 hours.

title: Tetracycline ↔ dairy
detail: Dairy calcium binds tetracycline and lowers antibiotic levels.
instruction: Take tetracycline 1 hour before or 2 hours after dairy meals.

title: Antibiotic ↔ probiotics
detail: Antibiotics can kill probiotic bacteria if taken at the same time.
instruction: Take probiotic at least 2 hours apart from antibiotic doses.

title: Cyclosporine ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort induces metabolism and can drastically lower cyclosporine levels.
instruction: Avoid St. John's Wort with transplant medications unless transplant team approves.

title: Cyclosporine ↔ grapefruit
detail: Grapefruit inhibits metabolism and can raise cyclosporine to toxic levels.
instruction: Avoid grapefruit completely with cyclosporine unless transplant team directs otherwise.

title: Tacrolimus ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort can lower tacrolimus concentrations and risk transplant rejection.
instruction: Do not use St. John's Wort with tacrolimus.

title: Immunosuppressant ↔ echinacea
detail: Echinacea may stimulate immune activity and oppose immunosuppressive therapy.
instruction: Avoid echinacea with transplant or autoimmune immunosuppression unless approved.

title: Levodopa ↔ iron
detail: Iron chelates levodopa in the gut and reduces absorption.
instruction: Separate levodopa and iron by at least 2 hours.

title: Levodopa ↔ high-dose vitamin B6
detail: High-dose vitamin B6 can accelerate peripheral metabolism of levodopa and reduce effect.
instruction: Avoid high-dose B6 unless your neurologist prescribes it.

title: Levodopa ↔ protein supplements
detail: Large protein loads compete for brain uptake of levodopa.
instruction: Time protein supplements away from levodopa per your movement-disorder specialist.

title: Calcium ↔ iron
detail: Calcium inhibits iron absorption when taken together.
instruction: Take iron and calcium at least 2 hours apart.
source: NIH ODS iron / NIH ODS calcium

title: Calcium ↔ magnesium (high dose)
detail: At typical supplement doses calcium and magnesium compete minimally for absorption. Very high combined doses may reduce uptake of both.
instruction: At standard doses this is not a significant concern. If taking high doses of both, split across meals.

title: Zinc ↔ copper
detail: Long-term high-dose zinc can deplete copper and cause deficiency.
instruction: Discuss zinc dose and copper monitoring with your clinician for prolonged use.

title: Zinc ↔ iron
detail: Zinc and iron compete for absorption when taken together.
instruction: Separate zinc and iron by at least 2 hours.

title: Iron ↔ vitamin C
detail: Vitamin C enhances non-heme iron absorption when taken together.
instruction: Pair iron with vitamin C for absorption unless your clinician advises otherwise.

title: Benzodiazepine ↔ melatonin
detail: Melatonin may add to sedation with benzodiazepines.
instruction: Use lowest doses; avoid alcohol; do not drive if impaired.

title: Benzodiazepine ↔ valerian
detail: Valerian may increase sedation with benzodiazepines.
instruction: Avoid combining sedative herbs with benzodiazepines unless approved.

title: Benzodiazepine ↔ kava
detail: Kava adds sedation and carries liver toxicity risk, worsened with CNS depressants.
instruction: Avoid kava with benzodiazepines.

title: Zolpidem ↔ melatonin
detail: Melatonin may increase sedation with sedative-hypnotics.
instruction: Use cautiously; avoid alcohol the same night.

title: Bisphosphonate ↔ calcium
detail: Calcium and food substantially reduce bisphosphonate absorption.
instruction: Take bisphosphonate on empty stomach with plain water; delay calcium per label instructions.

title: Bisphosphonate ↔ iron
detail: Iron and minerals reduce bisphosphonate absorption.
instruction: Do not take iron within several hours of oral bisphosphonate.

title: Bisphosphonate ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium reduces bisphosphonate absorption.
instruction: Separate magnesium from oral bisphosphonate by several hours.

title: Lithium ↔ low sodium / salt restriction
detail: Sodium and lithium compete for kidney reabsorption. Low sodium intake causes lithium to accumulate to potentially toxic levels.
instruction: Never start a low-sodium diet or use salt substitutes without telling your prescriber. Maintain consistent sodium intake. Report tremor, confusion, or nausea immediately.

title: Lithium ↔ high-dose caffeine
detail: Sudden changes in caffeine intake can shift lithium levels — caffeine increases kidney lithium clearance.
instruction: Keep caffeine intake consistent. Do not suddenly start or stop high caffeine use while on lithium.

title: Lithium ↔ magnesium
detail: Magnesium and lithium share some renal handling pathways. High-dose magnesium may affect lithium clearance in some patients.
instruction: Discuss magnesium supplementation with your prescriber if you take lithium. Keep your psychiatry team informed of all supplements.

title: MAOI ↔ 5-HTP
detail: 5-HTP combined with MAOIs can cause severe serotonin syndrome — a potentially life-threatening condition with fever, seizures, and cardiovascular instability.
instruction: Never combine 5-HTP with MAOIs. Seek emergency care for agitation, fever, rigid muscles, or rapid heart rate.

title: MAOI ↔ tryptophan
detail: Tryptophan with MAOIs can precipitate serotonin syndrome.
instruction: Do not combine tryptophan supplements with MAOIs under any circumstances.

title: MAOI ↔ tyrosine
detail: Tyrosine is a precursor to tyramine. MAOIs block tyramine breakdown and combined with tyrosine supplements can trigger hypertensive crisis.
instruction: Avoid tyrosine and phenylalanine supplements with MAOIs. Report sudden severe headache immediately — this is a medical emergency.

title: MAOI ↔ ginseng
detail: Ginseng may have serotonergic and stimulant properties that interact with MAOIs.
instruction: Avoid ginseng supplements with MAOIs unless your psychiatrist approves.

title: Digoxin ↔ hawthorn
detail: Hawthorn has cardiac glycoside-like activity and can add to digoxin's effects, potentially causing toxicity.
instruction: Do not combine hawthorn with digoxin without cardiology approval. Report nausea, visual disturbance, or irregular heartbeat immediately.

title: Digoxin ↔ magnesium
detail: Low magnesium increases digoxin toxicity risk. High-dose magnesium supplements can affect cardiac conduction in digoxin-treated patients.
instruction: Maintain adequate magnesium but avoid high-dose supplementation without cardiology guidance. Report palpitations or visual changes.

title: Digoxin ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort induces P-glycoprotein and CYP enzymes, significantly reducing digoxin levels and risking loss of cardiac control.
instruction: Do not use St. John's Wort with digoxin.

title: Phenytoin ↔ folic acid
detail: Phenytoin lowers folate levels. However high-dose folic acid can also lower phenytoin levels and increase seizure risk.
instruction: Take only the folic acid dose your neurologist recommends. Do not self-supplement with high-dose folate on phenytoin.

title: Phenytoin ↔ vitamin D
detail: Phenytoin accelerates vitamin D metabolism and can cause deficiency over time, leading to bone loss.
instruction: Discuss vitamin D monitoring and supplementation with your neurologist. Annual bone density review is advisable for long-term phenytoin users.
source: FDA label / NIH ODS vitamin D

title: Phenytoin ↔ calcium
detail: Calcium can reduce phenytoin absorption when taken at the same time.
instruction: Separate calcium supplements from phenytoin by at least 2 hours.

title: Corticosteroid ↔ calcium + vitamin D
detail: Corticosteroids deplete calcium and interfere with vitamin D metabolism, significantly increasing osteoporosis risk with long-term use.
instruction: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation is generally recommended for patients on long-term corticosteroids. Discuss doses with your prescriber.
source: NIH ODS calcium / NIH ODS vitamin D / ACR guidelines
monitor: bone density, calcium, vitamin D level

title: Corticosteroid ↔ potassium
detail: Corticosteroids cause potassium loss through the kidneys. Low potassium can cause muscle weakness and heart rhythm problems.
instruction: Discuss potassium monitoring with your clinician if on long-term steroids. Eat potassium-rich foods or supplement as directed.

title: Corticosteroid ↔ zinc
detail: Long-term corticosteroid use may increase zinc excretion and contribute to deficiency over time.
instruction: Discuss zinc monitoring with your clinician if on long-term steroids.

title: Clopidogrel ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 fatty acids have antiplatelet properties that may add to clopidogrel's blood-thinning effects.
instruction: Discuss fish oil dose with your cardiologist. Report unusual bruising or bleeding.

title: Clopidogrel ↔ vitamin E
detail: High-dose vitamin E has antiplatelet activity and may add to clopidogrel's effect.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin E with antiplatelet therapy unless your cardiologist approves.

title: Clopidogrel ↔ ginkgo biloba
detail: Ginkgo inhibits platelet aggregation and can add to clopidogrel's antiplatelet effect.
instruction: Avoid ginkgo with antiplatelet medications. Report bleeding or unusual bruising.

title: Tamoxifen ↔ black cohosh
detail: Black cohosh may have oestrogenic activity and could interfere with tamoxifen's mechanism in oestrogen-sensitive cancers.
instruction: Do not use black cohosh with tamoxifen without explicit oncology approval.
source: NIH NCCIH black cohosh / oncology literature

title: Tamoxifen ↔ soy isoflavones
detail: Soy isoflavones are phytoestrogens that may theoretically interfere with tamoxifen in oestrogen receptor-positive cancers.
instruction: Discuss soy supplement use with your oncologist. Dietary soy is generally considered different from concentrated isoflavone supplements.

title: Methotrexate ↔ folic acid
detail: Methotrexate works partly by blocking folate pathways. Supplemental folate is often prescribed to reduce side effects but must be dosed carefully — too much may reduce methotrexate's effectiveness.
instruction: Only take the folic acid dose your rheumatologist or oncologist prescribes. Do not self-supplement beyond the recommended amount.

title: Methotrexate ↔ high-dose vitamin C
detail: High-dose vitamin C may reduce kidney clearance of methotrexate and increase toxicity risk.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin C supplements with methotrexate. Discuss any supplementation with your rheumatologist.

title: HIV medication ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort potently induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, dramatically reducing antiretroviral drug levels and risking HIV resistance.
instruction: St. John's Wort is absolutely contraindicated with antiretroviral therapy. This is not a 'discuss with your doctor' situation — do not combine.

title: HIV medication ↔ high-dose garlic
detail: High-dose garlic supplements may reduce levels of some protease inhibitors through enzyme induction.
instruction: Discuss garlic supplement use with your HIV specialist. Dietary garlic is generally not a concern at normal food amounts.
source: NIH NCCIH garlic / HIV pharmacology literature

title: ADHD stimulant ↔ vitamin C
detail: Vitamin C acidifies urine and accelerates elimination of amphetamine-based ADHD medications, reducing their duration and effectiveness.
instruction: Avoid large vitamin C doses or acidic drinks around the time of stimulant doses. Take vitamin C at a different time of day.

title: ADHD stimulant ↔ zinc
detail: Zinc deficiency has been associated with poorer ADHD symptom control. Some evidence suggests zinc may modulate stimulant response.
instruction: Discuss zinc status with your prescriber. Do not self-supplement zinc as a substitute for prescribed medication.
source: NIH ODS zinc / ADHD clinical literature

title: Antipsychotic ↔ melatonin
detail: Melatonin may add to sedation with antipsychotics. Some antipsychotics already affect melatonin pathways.
instruction: Use lowest effective melatonin dose. Avoid driving or operating machinery if sedation increases.

title: Antipsychotic ↔ high caffeine
detail: High caffeine intake can interact with some antipsychotics — caffeine inhibits adenosine receptors and may affect drug metabolism.
instruction: Avoid very high caffeine intake with antipsychotics. Keep intake consistent.

title: Spironolactone ↔ potassium
detail: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic. Added potassium supplements can cause dangerous hyperkalemia — high potassium can stop the heart.
instruction: Do not take potassium supplements or potassium-containing salt substitutes with spironolactone unless your doctor prescribes them. Report muscle weakness, numbness, or irregular heartbeat immediately.

title: Spironolactone ↔ licorice root
detail: Licorice has mineralocorticoid-like activity and opposes spironolactone's mechanism, potentially causing sodium retention and potassium loss.
instruction: Avoid licorice supplements with spironolactone.

title: Hormonal contraceptive ↔ St. John's Wort
detail: St. John's Wort induces liver enzymes that break down contraceptive hormones, reducing their levels and risking breakthrough bleeding and unintended pregnancy.
instruction: Use a reliable backup method or avoid St. John's Wort while on hormonal contraception. Discuss alternatives with your prescriber.

title: Diuretic ↔ licorice root
detail: Licorice promotes potassium loss and sodium retention, worsening the potassium depletion caused by loop and thiazide diuretics — raising arrhythmia risk.
instruction: Avoid licorice supplements with diuretics. Report muscle cramps, weakness, or palpitations.

title: Thiazide diuretic ↔ calcium + vitamin D
detail: Thiazides reduce calcium excretion. With high-dose calcium plus vitamin D this can raise blood calcium to abnormal levels (hypercalcemia).
instruction: Keep calcium and vitamin D within recommended amounts and tell your clinician. Report nausea, constipation, or confusion.

title: Loop/thiazide diuretic ↔ magnesium
detail: Loop and thiazide diuretics increase magnesium loss; supplementation is often appropriate but should be guided by lab values.
instruction: Discuss magnesium monitoring with your clinician. Magnesium supplementation is commonly recommended rather than avoided.

title: Aspirin ↔ omega-3
detail: Omega-3 has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to aspirin's blood-thinning effect, especially at high fish-oil doses.
instruction: Low-dose aspirin with moderate fish oil is common, but tell your clinician and report unusual bruising or bleeding.

title: Aspirin ↔ ginkgo biloba
detail: Ginkgo inhibits platelet aggregation and combined with aspirin can meaningfully increase bleeding risk, including rare reports of intracranial bleeding.
instruction: Avoid ginkgo with aspirin. Report any unusual bruising, nosebleeds, or bleeding.

title: Iron ↔ coffee / tea (tannins)
detail: Tannins and polyphenols in coffee and tea bind non-heme iron and can substantially reduce its absorption when taken together.
instruction: Separate iron supplements from coffee or tea by at least 1–2 hours. Pairing iron with vitamin C improves absorption.

title: Aspirin ↔ high-dose garlic
detail: High-dose garlic supplements have mild antiplatelet activity that can add to aspirin's effect on bleeding.
instruction: Normal dietary garlic is fine. Discuss high-dose garlic supplements with your clinician and report unusual bruising or bleeding.

title: Aspirin ↔ high-dose vitamin E
detail: High-dose vitamin E has antiplatelet activity that may add to aspirin and increase bleeding risk.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin E with regular aspirin unless your clinician approves.

title: SSRI/SNRI ↔ ginkgo biloba
detail: SSRIs and SNRIs can impair platelet function; ginkgo adds antiplatelet activity, modestly increasing bleeding risk.
instruction: Discuss ginkgo use with your prescriber. Report unusual bruising, nosebleeds, or bleeding.

title: Loop/thiazide diuretic ↔ potassium
detail: Unlike potassium-sparing diuretics, loop and thiazide diuretics cause potassium loss. Supplemental potassium is sometimes prescribed — but only under guidance.
instruction: Take potassium only as directed by your clinician, who will monitor your levels. Do not start potassium supplements on your own.

title: Orlistat ↔ Vitamin D — Reduced Absorption
detail: Orlistat, together with a reduced-fat diet, can reduce the absorption of vitamin D from food and supplements, leading to lower vitamin D levels in the body.
instruction: Monitor vitamin D levels if taking orlistat regularly. Consider vitamin D supplementation and discuss intake with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Orlistat blocks fat absorption, which impairs absorption of fat-soluble vitamin D.

title: Statins ↔ Vitamin D — Reduced Synthesis
detail: Statin medications reduce cholesterol synthesis. Because vitamin D is derived from cholesterol, statins may also reduce vitamin D synthesis in the body.
instruction: Monitor vitamin D levels when taking statins. Discuss vitamin D supplementation needs with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Statins reduce cholesterol synthesis, which is the precursor for endogenous vitamin D production.

title: Atorvastatin ↔ Vitamin D — Reduced Statin Potency
detail: High intakes of vitamin D, especially from supplements, might reduce the effectiveness of atorvastatin because they compete for the same metabolizing enzyme.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin D supplements without medical supervision if taking atorvastatin. Discuss appropriate vitamin D dosing with your doctor.
mechanism: Atorvastatin and vitamin D compete for the same metabolizing enzyme in the liver.

title: Lovastatin ↔ Vitamin D — Reduced Statin Potency
detail: High intakes of vitamin D, especially from supplements, might reduce the effectiveness of lovastatin because they compete for the same metabolizing enzyme.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin D supplements without medical supervision if taking lovastatin. Discuss appropriate vitamin D dosing with your doctor.
mechanism: Lovastatin and vitamin D compete for the same metabolizing enzyme in the liver.

title: Simvastatin ↔ Vitamin D — Reduced Statin Potency
detail: High intakes of vitamin D, especially from supplements, might reduce the effectiveness of simvastatin because they compete for the same metabolizing enzyme.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin D supplements without medical supervision if taking simvastatin. Discuss appropriate vitamin D dosing with your doctor.
mechanism: Simvastatin and vitamin D compete for the same metabolizing enzyme in the liver.

title: Bisphosphonates ↔ Magnesium — reduced absorption
detail: Magnesium-rich supplements or medications can decrease the absorption of oral bisphosphonates such as alendronate (Fosamax) used to treat osteoporosis.
instruction: Take magnesium supplements or medications at least 2 hours before or after oral bisphosphonates to minimize this interaction.
mechanism: Magnesium forms complexes that interfere with bisphosphonate absorption.

title: Tetracyclines ↔ Magnesium — reduced antibiotic absorption
detail: Magnesium can form insoluble complexes with tetracyclines such as demeclocycline (Declomycin) and doxycycline (Vibramycin), reducing antibiotic effectiveness.
instruction: Take these antibiotics at least 2 hours before or 4 to 6 hours after a magnesium-containing supplement.
mechanism: Magnesium forms insoluble complexes with tetracyclines.

title: Quinolones ↔ Magnesium — reduced antibiotic absorption
detail: Magnesium can form insoluble complexes with quinolone antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin (Cipro) and levofloxacin (Levaquin), reducing antibiotic effectiveness.
instruction: Take these antibiotics at least 2 hours before or 4 to 6 hours after a magnesium-containing supplement.
mechanism: Magnesium forms insoluble complexes with quinolone antibiotics.

title: Potassium-Sparing Diuretics ↔ Magnesium — reduced magnesium loss
detail: Potassium-sparing diuretics such as amiloride (Midamor) and spironolactone (Aldactone) reduce magnesium excretion, helping to preserve magnesium levels.
instruction: Inform your healthcare provider about magnesium supplementation to avoid potential excessive magnesium levels.
mechanism: Potassium-sparing diuretics reduce urinary magnesium excretion.

title: Proton Pump Inhibitors ↔ Magnesium — magnesium depletion
detail: Prescription PPIs such as esomeprazole magnesium (Nexium) and lansoprazole (Prevacid) can cause hypomagnesemia when taken for prolonged periods (typically more than a year).
instruction: Healthcare providers should measure serum magnesium levels before starting long-term PPI treatment and check periodically. Supplements may help but in 25% of cases patients must discontinue the PPI.
mechanism: Prolonged PPI use can cause magnesium depletion.

title: Dolutegravir ↔ Calcium — Reduced Drug Absorption
detail: Calcium supplements can substantially reduce blood levels of dolutegravir through chelation, potentially reducing the effectiveness of this HIV medication.
instruction: Take dolutegravir 2 hours before or 6 hours after taking calcium supplements.
mechanism: Chelation between calcium and dolutegravir reduces drug absorption

title: Lithium ↔ Calcium — Increased Hypercalcemia Risk
detail: Long-term use of lithium can lead to hypercalcemia. Using both lithium and calcium supplements together could increase the risk of elevated calcium levels.
instruction: Monitor calcium levels if using lithium long-term with calcium supplements. Discuss with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Lithium can cause hypercalcemia, which may be compounded by calcium supplementation

title: Gemifloxacin ↔ Calcium — Reduced Antibiotic Absorption
detail: Simultaneous use of calcium supplements and gemifloxacin can reduce the absorption of the antibiotic, potentially decreasing its effectiveness against bacterial infections.
instruction: Take gemifloxacin 2 hours before or 2 hours after calcium supplements.
mechanism: Calcium reduces quinolone antibiotic absorption

title: Quinolone Antibiotics ↔ Calcium — Reduced Antibiotic Absorption
detail: Simultaneous use of calcium supplements and quinolone antibiotics can reduce the absorption of these antibiotics, potentially decreasing their effectiveness.
instruction: Take quinolone antibiotics 2 hours before or 2 hours after calcium supplements.
mechanism: Calcium reduces quinolone antibiotic absorption

title: Quinolone Antibiotics ↔ Zinc — Reduced Absorption
detail: Zinc interacts with quinolone antibiotics (such as Cipro) in the gastrointestinal tract, which can inhibit the absorption of both zinc and the antibiotic when taken together.
instruction: Take the antibiotic at least 2 hours before or 4-6 hours after the zinc supplement to minimize this interaction.
mechanism: Zinc binds with quinolone antibiotics in the GI tract, preventing absorption of both substances.

title: Tetracycline Antibiotics ↔ Zinc — Reduced Absorption
detail: Zinc interacts with tetracycline antibiotics (such as Achromycin and Sumycin) in the gastrointestinal tract, which can inhibit the absorption of both zinc and the antibiotic when taken simultaneously.
instruction: Take the antibiotic at least 2 hours before or 4-6 hours after the zinc supplement to minimize this interaction.
mechanism: Zinc binds with tetracycline antibiotics in the GI tract, preventing absorption of both substances.

title: Penicillamine ↔ Zinc — Reduced Drug Effectiveness
detail: Zinc can reduce the absorption and action of penicillamine, a drug used to treat rheumatoid arthritis and Wilson disease.
instruction: Take zinc supplements and penicillamine at least 1 hour apart to minimize this interaction.
mechanism: Zinc interferes with the absorption and pharmacological action of penicillamine.

title: Thiazide Diuretics ↔ Zinc — Decreased Zinc Levels
detail: Thiazide diuretics, such as chlorthalidone and hydrochlorothiazide, increase zinc excretion in the urine, which decreases serum zinc concentrations.
instruction: Monitor zinc status with your healthcare provider if taking thiazide diuretics regularly. Consider zinc supplementation if deficiency develops.
mechanism: Thiazide diuretics increase urinary zinc excretion, leading to lower blood zinc levels.

title: Proton Pump Inhibitors ↔ Iron — Reduced Iron Absorption
detail: PPIs reduce stomach acidity, which can reduce nonheme iron absorption. Patients with iron deficiency taking PPIs can have suboptimal responses to iron supplementation.
instruction: If you have iron deficiency and take PPIs, monitor iron levels regularly. Discuss alternative iron formulations or PPI alternatives with your doctor.
mechanism: PPIs reduce gastric acid production, which is necessary for nonheme iron absorption from the diet and supplements.

title: Vitamin C ↔ Cyclophosphamide — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Chlorambucil — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Carmustine — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Busulfan — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Thiotepa — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Doxorubicin — may reduce effectiveness
detail: Antioxidants like vitamin C might protect tumor cells from the action of chemotherapeutic agents, potentially reducing treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C supplements during chemotherapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells from chemotherapy-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Radiation Therapy — controversial interaction
detail: Some data indicate antioxidants might protect tumor cells from radiation, though other data suggest they may protect normal tissues or enhance treatment effectiveness.
instruction: Consult with your oncologist prior to taking vitamin C or other antioxidant supplements during radiation therapy, especially in high doses.
mechanism: Antioxidants may protect tumor cells or normal tissues from radiation-induced damage

title: Vitamin C ↔ Statins — may reduce HDL benefits
detail: Vitamin C combined with other antioxidants may attenuate the increase in HDL cholesterol levels from niacin/simvastatin combination therapy.
instruction: Health care providers should monitor lipid levels in individuals taking both statins and antioxidant supplements.
mechanism: Antioxidants may interfere with the HDL-raising effects of statin/niacin combinations

title: Vitamin C ↔ Simvastatin — may reduce HDL benefits
detail: Vitamin C combined with other antioxidants may attenuate the increase in HDL cholesterol levels from niacin/simvastatin combination therapy.
instruction: Health care providers should monitor lipid levels when taking both simvastatin combinations and antioxidant supplements.
mechanism: Antioxidants may interfere with the HDL-raising effects of statin/niacin combinations

title: Vitamin C ↔ Niacin — may reduce HDL benefits
detail: Vitamin C combined with other antioxidants may attenuate the increase in HDL cholesterol levels from niacin/simvastatin combination therapy.
instruction: Health care providers should monitor lipid levels when taking niacin combinations with antioxidant supplements.
mechanism: Antioxidants may interfere with the HDL-raising effects of statin/niacin combinations

title: Anticoagulants ↔ Vitamin E — increased bleeding risk
detail: Vitamin E inhibits platelet aggregation and antagonizes vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Large doses (likely >400 IU/day) with anticoagulants increase bleeding risk.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin E supplements (>400 IU/day) while taking anticoagulants. Consult your doctor about safe vitamin E levels.
mechanism: Vitamin E inhibits platelet aggregation and antagonizes vitamin K-dependent clotting factors

title: Antiplatelet Drugs ↔ Vitamin E — increased bleeding risk
detail: Vitamin E inhibits platelet aggregation. Large doses (likely >400 IU/day) with antiplatelet medications increase bleeding risk, especially with low vitamin K intake.
instruction: Avoid high-dose vitamin E supplements (>400 IU/day) while taking antiplatelet drugs. Discuss vitamin E use with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Vitamin E inhibits platelet aggregation

title: Simvastatin-Niacin ↔ Vitamin E — reduced HDL benefit
detail: Vitamin E with other antioxidants blunted the rise in HDL cholesterol, especially cardioprotective HDL2 levels, in people treated with simvastatin and niacin combination therapy.
instruction: Avoid taking vitamin E with other antioxidants if you're on simvastatin-niacin therapy. Inform your doctor about any antioxidant supplements.
mechanism: Antioxidant combination interferes with HDL cholesterol response to statin-niacin therapy

title: Chemotherapy ↔ Vitamin E — may reduce treatment effectiveness
detail: Antioxidant supplements including vitamin E might reduce chemotherapy effectiveness by inhibiting cellular oxidative damage in cancerous cells, which is part of how chemotherapy works.
instruction: Avoid vitamin E and other antioxidant supplements during chemotherapy unless approved by your oncologist. Discuss any supplement use with your cancer care team.
mechanism: Antioxidants may inhibit cellular oxidative damage in cancerous cells that chemotherapy relies on

title: Radiotherapy ↔ Vitamin E — may reduce treatment effectiveness
detail: Antioxidant supplements including vitamin E might reduce radiotherapy effectiveness by inhibiting cellular oxidative damage in cancerous cells, which radiation therapy relies on.
instruction: Avoid vitamin E and other antioxidant supplements during radiotherapy unless approved by your oncologist. Consult your cancer care team before using any supplements.
mechanism: Antioxidants may inhibit cellular oxidative damage in cancerous cells that radiotherapy relies on

title: Phenprocoumon ↔ Vitamin K — antagonizes anticoagulant effect
detail: Vitamin K can seriously interfere with phenprocoumon's anticoagulant activity. Sudden changes in vitamin K intake can increase or decrease the anticoagulant effect.
instruction: Maintain consistent vitamin K intake from food and supplements. Consult your doctor before changing vitamin K consumption.
mechanism: Phenprocoumon antagonizes vitamin K activity, depleting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.

title: Acenocoumarol ↔ Vitamin K — antagonizes anticoagulant effect
detail: Vitamin K can seriously interfere with acenocoumarol's anticoagulant activity. Sudden changes in vitamin K intake can increase or decrease the anticoagulant effect.
instruction: Maintain consistent vitamin K intake from food and supplements. Consult your doctor before changing vitamin K consumption.
mechanism: Acenocoumarol antagonizes vitamin K activity, depleting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.

title: Tioclomarol ↔ Vitamin K — antagonizes anticoagulant effect
detail: Vitamin K can seriously interfere with tioclomarol's anticoagulant activity. Sudden changes in vitamin K intake can increase or decrease the anticoagulant effect.
instruction: Maintain consistent vitamin K intake from food and supplements. Consult your doctor before changing vitamin K consumption.
mechanism: Tioclomarol antagonizes vitamin K activity, depleting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.

title: Antibiotics ↔ Vitamin K — may deplete vitamin K levels
detail: Antibiotics can destroy vitamin K-producing bacteria in the gut, potentially decreasing vitamin K status. Usually not a concern unless antibiotic use is prolonged beyond several weeks.
instruction: Monitor vitamin K status if taking antibiotics for extended periods (beyond several weeks) with poor vitamin K intake.
mechanism: Antibiotics destroy vitamin K-producing bacteria in the gut.

title: Cefoperazone ↔ Vitamin K — depletes and inhibits vitamin K
detail: Cefoperazone can destroy vitamin K-producing gut bacteria and also directly inhibit vitamin K activity in the body, creating a more pronounced effect than other antibiotics.
instruction: Monitor vitamin K status closely. May need supplementation if use is prolonged, especially with poor dietary vitamin K intake.
mechanism: Destroys vitamin K-producing bacteria and inhibits vitamin K action in the body.

title: Cephalosporins ↔ Vitamin K — depletes and inhibits vitamin K
detail: Cephalosporin antibiotics can destroy vitamin K-producing gut bacteria and may also inhibit vitamin K action in the body, potentially causing more pronounced vitamin K depletion.
instruction: Monitor vitamin K status closely. May need supplementation if use is prolonged, especially with poor dietary vitamin K intake.
mechanism: Destroys vitamin K-producing bacteria and may inhibit vitamin K action in the body.

title: Cholestyramine ↔ Vitamin K — reduces vitamin K absorption
detail: Cholestyramine can reduce absorption of vitamin K and other fat-soluble vitamins. The clinical significance is unclear but may be important with long-term use.
instruction: Monitor vitamin K status if taking cholestyramine for many years. Consider vitamin K supplementation with prolonged use.
mechanism: Bile acid sequestrants prevent reabsorption of bile acids, which can reduce fat-soluble vitamin absorption.

title: Colestipol ↔ Vitamin K — reduces vitamin K absorption
detail: Colestipol can reduce absorption of vitamin K and other fat-soluble vitamins. The clinical significance is unclear but may be important with long-term use.
instruction: Monitor vitamin K status if taking colestipol for many years. Consider vitamin K supplementation with prolonged use.
mechanism: Bile acid sequestrants prevent reabsorption of bile acids, which can reduce fat-soluble vitamin absorption.

title: Orlistat ↔ Vitamin K — reduces vitamin K absorption
detail: Orlistat reduces dietary fat absorption and can also reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins like vitamin K. Usually not clinically significant, but multivitamin supplementation is recommended.
instruction: Take a multivitamin containing vitamin K while using orlistat. Inform your doctor if also taking warfarin, as combination may significantly affect clotting.
mechanism: Orlistat blocks dietary fat absorption, which reduces absorption of fat-soluble vitamins.

title: Proton Pump Inhibitors ↔ Vitamin B12 — Reduced Absorption
detail: PPIs like omeprazole and lansoprazole slow gastric acid release, interfering with vitamin B12 absorption from food and potentially leading to deficiency.
instruction: Monitor vitamin B12 levels regularly if taking PPIs long-term. Discuss B12 supplementation with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Reduced gastric acid decreases the release of vitamin B12 from food proteins in the stomach.

title: H2 Blockers ↔ Vitamin B12 — Reduced Absorption
detail: H2 blockers like cimetidine and ranitidine slow gastric acid release, interfering with vitamin B12 absorption from food and potentially leading to deficiency.
instruction: Monitor vitamin B12 levels regularly if taking H2 blockers long-term. Discuss B12 supplementation with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Reduced gastric acid decreases the release of vitamin B12 from food proteins in the stomach.

title: Carbamazepine ↔ Folate — Bidirectional interaction affects both levels
detail: Carbamazepine can reduce serum folate levels, causing potential deficiency. Conversely, folate supplements might reduce serum levels of carbamazepine, potentially decreasing therapeutic effectiveness.
instruction: Check with your healthcare provider before taking folate supplements if you take carbamazepine for epilepsy, psychiatric conditions, or other conditions.
mechanism: Antiepileptic medications reduce folate absorption or increase metabolism; folate may enhance drug metabolism

title: Valproate ↔ Folate — Bidirectional interaction affects both levels
detail: Valproate can reduce serum folate levels, causing potential deficiency. Conversely, folate supplements might reduce serum levels of valproate, potentially decreasing seizure control or mood stabilization.
instruction: Check with your healthcare provider before taking folate supplements if you take valproate for epilepsy, psychiatric conditions, or other conditions.
mechanism: Antiepileptic medications reduce folate absorption or increase metabolism; folate may enhance drug metabolism

title: Sulfasalazine ↔ Folate — Drug inhibits folate absorption
detail: Sulfasalazine inhibits intestinal absorption of folate and can cause folate deficiency over time, particularly in patients with ulcerative colitis requiring long-term treatment.
instruction: Ask your healthcare provider whether you should increase dietary folate intake or start a folate supplement while taking sulfasalazine.
mechanism: Sulfasalazine inhibits intestinal folate absorption through competitive inhibition of folate transporters

title: Cycloserine ↔ Vitamin B6 — Increased urinary loss
detail: Cycloserine increases urinary excretion of pyridoxine, which might worsen seizures and neurotoxicity associated with the antibiotic.
instruction: Take pyridoxine supplements to help prevent adverse effects. Discuss supplementation with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Cycloserine in combination with pyridoxal phosphate increases urinary excretion of pyridoxine

title: Valproic Acid ↔ Vitamin B6 — Depletes B6 levels
detail: Valproic acid increases catabolism of vitamin B6, resulting in low plasma PLP concentrations and elevated homocysteine levels.
instruction: Monitor vitamin B6 status regularly. Elevated homocysteine may increase seizure risk and cardiovascular events.
mechanism: Antiepileptic drugs increase the catabolism rate of vitamin B6 vitamers

title: Carbamazepine ↔ Vitamin B6 — Depletes B6 levels
detail: Carbamazepine increases catabolism of vitamin B6, resulting in low plasma PLP concentrations and elevated homocysteine levels.
instruction: Monitor vitamin B6 status regularly. Elevated homocysteine may increase seizure risk and cardiovascular events.
mechanism: Antiepileptic drugs increase the catabolism rate of vitamin B6 vitamers

title: Phenytoin ↔ Vitamin B6 — Bidirectional interaction
detail: Phenytoin depletes vitamin B6, while high-dose pyridoxine (200mg/day) can reduce phenytoin levels by increasing drug metabolism.
instruction: Consult your doctor before taking vitamin B6 supplements. High doses may reduce seizure control effectiveness.
mechanism: Pyridoxine supplementation increases the drugs' metabolism

title: Phenobarbital ↔ Vitamin B6 — Reduced drug levels
detail: High-dose pyridoxine supplementation (200mg/day for 12-120 days) can reduce serum phenobarbital concentrations by increasing drug metabolism.
instruction: Consult your doctor before taking vitamin B6 supplements. High doses may reduce seizure control effectiveness.
mechanism: Pyridoxine supplementation increases the drugs' metabolism

title: Levetiracetam ↔ Vitamin B6 — May reduce side effects
detail: Preliminary evidence suggests vitamin B6 (50-350mg/day in children, 50-100mg/day in adults) might reduce behavioral side effects like irritability, agitation, and depression.
instruction: Inform your doctor if considering B6 supplementation for behavioral side effects.

title: Theophylline ↔ Vitamin B6 — Depletes B6 levels
detail: Theophylline treatment often causes low plasma PLP concentrations, which could contribute to neurological and CNS side effects including seizures.
instruction: Monitor vitamin B6 status if using theophylline long-term. Consider supplementation to prevent neurological side effects.

title: Orlistat ↔ Vitamin A — decreased absorption
detail: Orlistat can decrease the absorption of vitamin A and other fat-soluble vitamins, resulting in low plasma levels in some patients.
instruction: Take a multivitamin supplement containing vitamin A and beta-carotene while on orlistat treatment as recommended by manufacturers.
mechanism: Orlistat blocks fat absorption, which reduces absorption of fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A.

title: Acitretin ↔ Vitamin A — hypervitaminosis A risk
detail: Acitretin, a synthetic retinoid used for psoriasis treatment, can increase the risk of hypervitaminosis A when taken in combination with vitamin A supplements.
instruction: Avoid vitamin A supplements or use only under medical supervision while taking acitretin to prevent vitamin A toxicity.
mechanism: Both acitretin and vitamin A are retinoids, leading to additive effects and potential toxicity.

title: Bexarotene ↔ Vitamin A — hypervitaminosis A risk
detail: Bexarotene, used to treat skin effects of T-cell lymphoma, can increase the risk of hypervitaminosis A when taken in combination with vitamin A supplements.
instruction: Avoid vitamin A supplements or use only under medical supervision while taking bexarotene to prevent vitamin A toxicity.
mechanism: Both bexarotene and vitamin A are retinoids, leading to additive effects and potential toxicity.

title: Retinoids ↔ Vitamin A — hypervitaminosis A risk
detail: Synthetic retinoids derived from vitamin A can increase the risk of hypervitaminosis A when taken in combination with vitamin A supplements.
instruction: Avoid vitamin A supplements or consult your healthcare provider if taking any retinoid medication to prevent vitamin A toxicity.
mechanism: Retinoids are vitamin A derivatives, leading to cumulative vitamin A effects and potential toxicity.

title: Anticoagulants ↔ Omega-3 — Monitor for Bleeding
detail: Omega-3 supplements can have antiplatelet effects at high doses. Studies have not produced clinically significant bleeding episodes, but monitoring is recommended when combined with anticoagulant medications.
instruction: Patients taking anticoagulants with omega-3 products should be monitored periodically for changes in clotting status. Consult your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Fish oil has antiplatelet effects that may theoretically enhance anticoagulant activity.

title: Immunosuppressants ↔ Probiotics — infection risk
detail: Immunosuppressed patients may be at increased risk of serious infections from live probiotic microorganisms. The immune system's reduced ability to control microbial growth could allow probiotic organisms to cause systemic infections.
instruction: Consult your doctor before using probiotics if you are taking immunosuppressant medications or have a compromised immune system. Close monitoring may be necessary.
mechanism: Immunosuppression reduces the body's ability to control and clear live microorganisms, including those in probiotic supplements.

title: Cisplatin ↔ Selenium — reduced selenium levels
detail: Cisplatin chemotherapy can reduce selenium levels in hair and serum. The clinical significance of these reductions is not yet known.
instruction: Monitor selenium levels if taking cisplatin. Discuss supplementation with your oncologist before starting.
mechanism: Cisplatin depletes selenium through unclear mechanisms during cancer treatment

title: Selenium ↔ Cisplatin — potential toxicity reduction
detail: Some small studies suggest selenium supplementation may reduce cisplatin's toxicity, but a Cochrane Review found insufficient evidence that selenium alleviates chemotherapy side effects.
instruction: Inform your oncologist if considering selenium during cisplatin treatment. Evidence for benefit is currently insufficient.
mechanism: Selenium may have antioxidant effects that could theoretically protect against cisplatin toxicity

title: Methimazole ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hypothyroidism
detail: Taking high doses of iodine with methimazole can have an additive effect and could cause hypothyroidism by excessively suppressing thyroid function.
instruction: Discuss iodine intake with your healthcare provider if taking methimazole regularly. Avoid high-dose iodine supplements.
mechanism: Additive effect of both substances suppressing thyroid hormone production

title: Antithyroid Medications ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hypothyroidism
detail: Taking high doses of iodine with antithyroid medications can have an additive effect and could cause hypothyroidism.
instruction: Individuals taking antithyroid medications should discuss their iodine intakes with healthcare providers.
mechanism: Additive effect suppressing thyroid hormone production

title: Benazepril ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with benazepril can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Discuss iodine supplement use with your healthcare provider if taking benazepril. Monitor potassium levels regularly.
mechanism: Combined effect increasing blood potassium levels

title: Lisinopril ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with lisinopril can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Discuss iodine supplement use with your healthcare provider if taking lisinopril. Monitor potassium levels regularly.
mechanism: Combined effect increasing blood potassium levels

title: Fosinopril ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with fosinopril can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Discuss iodine supplement use with your healthcare provider if taking fosinopril. Monitor potassium levels regularly.
mechanism: Combined effect increasing blood potassium levels

title: ACE Inhibitors ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with ACE inhibitors can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Individuals taking ACE inhibitors should discuss their iodine intakes with healthcare providers and monitor potassium levels.
mechanism: Combined effect increasing blood potassium levels

title: Spironolactone ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with spironolactone can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Discuss iodine supplement use with your healthcare provider if taking spironolactone. Monitor potassium levels regularly.
mechanism: Combined effect of potassium-sparing diuretic and potassium iodide increasing blood potassium

title: Amiloride ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with amiloride can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Discuss iodine supplement use with your healthcare provider if taking amiloride. Monitor potassium levels regularly.
mechanism: Combined effect of potassium-sparing diuretic and potassium iodide increasing blood potassium

title: Potassium-Sparing Diuretics ↔ Iodine — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Taking potassium iodide with potassium-sparing diuretics can increase the risk of hyperkalemia (elevated blood levels of potassium).
instruction: Individuals taking potassium-sparing diuretics should discuss their iodine intakes with healthcare providers.
mechanism: Combined effect of potassium-sparing diuretic and potassium iodide increasing blood potassium

title: Insulin ↔ Chromium — increased hypoglycemia risk
detail: Chromium might increase insulin sensitivity. Taking chromium with insulin could increase the risk of hypoglycemia (low blood sugar).
instruction: Monitor blood glucose closely and consult your healthcare provider about potential dose adjustments.
mechanism: Chromium enhances insulin sensitivity, potentially amplifying insulin's glucose-lowering effects

title: Antidiabetes Medications ↔ Chromium — increased hypoglycemia risk
detail: Chromium supplementation might lower blood glucose levels. Chromium supplements might have an additive effect with antidiabetes medications and increase the risk of hypoglycemia.
instruction: Monitor blood sugar closely and consult your doctor before taking chromium with any diabetes medications.
mechanism: Chromium may enhance the glucose-lowering effects of antidiabetic drugs through additive mechanisms

title: Levothyroxine ↔ Chromium — decreased thyroid hormone absorption
detail: Taking chromium picolinate supplements at the same time as levothyroxine decreases levothyroxine absorption over 6 hours, potentially reducing thyroid medication effectiveness.
instruction: Separate chromium and levothyroxine doses by at least several hours to avoid absorption interference.
mechanism: Chromium picolinate interferes with levothyroxine absorption in the gastrointestinal tract

title: ARBs ↔ Potassium — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Angiotensin receptor blockers reduce urinary potassium excretion, which can lead to dangerously high potassium levels (hyperkalemia) when combined with potassium supplements.
instruction: Monitor potassium status regularly, especially if you have kidney disease or other risk factors. Consult your healthcare provider before taking potassium supplements.
mechanism: ARBs reduce urinary potassium excretion

title: Potassium-Sparing Diuretics ↔ Potassium — Risk of Hyperkalemia
detail: Potassium-sparing diuretics reduce the excretion of potassium in the urine and can cause hyperkalemia when combined with potassium supplements.
instruction: Monitor potassium status regularly, especially if you have impaired kidney function. Consult your healthcare provider before taking potassium supplements.
mechanism: These medications reduce urinary potassium excretion

title: Aluminum Hydroxide Antacids ↔ Phosphorus — chronic use depletes phosphate
detail: Aluminum hydroxide antacids (Maalox HRF, Rulox) bind phosphorus in the intestines. Chronic use for 3+ months can lead to hypophosphatemia and aggravate existing phosphate deficiency.
instruction: Avoid long-term use of aluminum hydroxide antacids. If needed, discuss phosphorus supplementation with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Aluminum hydroxide binds phosphorus in the intestinal tract, preventing absorption

title: Calcium Carbonate Antacids ↔ Phosphorus — reduces absorption
detail: Calcium carbonate antacids (Rolaids, Tums, Maalox) decrease intestinal absorption of dietary phosphorus.
instruction: Be aware that calcium carbonate antacids may reduce phosphorus absorption. Monitor phosphorus status if using regularly.
mechanism: Calcium carbonate interferes with intestinal absorption of phosphorus

title: Sodium Phosphate Laxatives ↔ Phosphorus — dangerous phosphate overload
detail: Sodium phosphate laxatives (Fleet Prep Kit #1) increase serum phosphate levels. Exceeding recommended doses can be dangerous, especially in kidney disease, heart disease, or dehydration. FDA warned of 13 deaths.
instruction: Never exceed recommended dose. Contraindicated if you have kidney disease, heart disease, or dehydration. Consult doctor before use.
mechanism: Sodium phosphate laxatives directly increase serum phosphate levels through intestinal absorption

title: Isoniazid ↔ Niacin — depletes niacin production
detail: Isoniazid is a structural analog of niacin that interrupts niacin production from tryptophan and interferes with niacin's conversion to NAD. Can cause pellagra in tuberculosis patients.
instruction: Increase niacin intake if taking isoniazid. Discuss niacin supplementation with your healthcare provider to prevent deficiency.
mechanism: Competes with vitamin B6-dependent enzyme required for niacin production from tryptophan and blocks conversion to NAD

title: Pyrazinamide ↔ Niacin — depletes niacin production
detail: Pyrazinamide is a structural analog of niacin that interrupts niacin production from tryptophan by competing with required enzymes. Can lead to pellagra in tuberculosis patients.
instruction: Increase niacin intake if taking pyrazinamide. Discuss niacin supplementation with your healthcare provider to prevent deficiency.
mechanism: Competes with vitamin B6-dependent enzyme required for niacin production from tryptophan

title: Antidiabetes Medications ↔ Niacin — raises blood glucose
detail: Large doses of nicotinic acid (1.5g/day or more) can raise blood glucose levels by causing or aggravating insulin resistance and increasing hepatic glucose production.
instruction: Have blood glucose monitored regularly if taking high-dose niacin with diabetes medications. Medication dose adjustments may be required.
mechanism: Causes or aggravates insulin resistance and increases hepatic production of glucose

title: Furosemide ↔ Thiamin — Depletes thiamin levels
detail: Furosemide (Lasix), a loop diuretic, decreases thiamin concentrations possibly to deficient levels through increased urinary thiamin loss.
instruction: Discuss thiamin status with healthcare provider if taking furosemide regularly. Thiamin supplementation may be needed to prevent deficiency.
mechanism: Loop diuretics increase urinary output and thiamin excretion in urine.

title: Fluorouracil ↔ Thiamin — Risk of beriberi/encephalopathy
detail: Fluorouracil (5-fluorouracil, Adrucil) chemotherapy has caused cases of beriberi or Wernicke's encephalopathy, possibly by increasing thiamin metabolism and blocking TDP formation.
instruction: Inform oncologist if receiving fluorouracil chemotherapy. Thiamin supplements might reverse these neurological effects.
mechanism: The drug may increase thiamin metabolism and block the formation of TDP, the active form of thiamin.

title: Carbamazepine ↔ Biotin — reduced biotin levels
detail: Long-term carbamazepine treatment (≥1 year) is associated with significantly lower serum biotin levels. The anticonvulsant increases biotin breakdown and inhibits intestinal absorption.
instruction: Monitor biotin status with your healthcare provider if taking carbamazepine long-term. Consider biotin supplementation if levels are low.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant increases biotin catabolism and inhibits intestinal biotin absorption

title: Primidone ↔ Biotin — reduced biotin levels
detail: Long-term primidone treatment (≥1 year) is associated with significantly lower serum biotin levels. The anticonvulsant increases biotin breakdown and inhibits intestinal absorption.
instruction: Monitor biotin status with your healthcare provider if taking primidone long-term. Consider biotin supplementation if levels are low.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant increases biotin catabolism and inhibits intestinal biotin absorption

title: Phenytoin ↔ Biotin — reduced biotin levels
detail: Long-term phenytoin treatment (≥1 year) is associated with significantly lower serum biotin levels. The anticonvulsant increases biotin breakdown and inhibits intestinal absorption.
instruction: Monitor biotin status with your healthcare provider if taking phenytoin long-term. Consider biotin supplementation if levels are low.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant increases biotin catabolism and inhibits intestinal biotin absorption

title: Phenobarbital ↔ Biotin — reduced biotin levels
detail: Long-term phenobarbital treatment (≥1 year) is associated with significantly lower serum biotin levels. The anticonvulsant increases biotin breakdown and inhibits intestinal absorption.
instruction: Monitor biotin status with your healthcare provider if taking phenobarbital long-term. Consider biotin supplementation if levels are low.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant increases biotin catabolism and inhibits intestinal biotin absorption

title: Pivampicillin ↔ Carnitine — Depletion Risk
detail: Chronic administration of pivalate-conjugated antibiotics like pivampicillin can lead to carnitine depletion and tissue levels low enough to limit fatty acid oxidation.
instruction: Monitor carnitine levels if using these antibiotics long-term. Discuss carnitine supplementation with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Pivalate-conjugated antibiotics interfere with carnitine metabolism leading to depletion

title: Pivalate Antibiotics ↔ Carnitine — Depletion
detail: Chronic use of pivalate-conjugated antibiotics for urinary tract infection prevention can deplete carnitine levels, potentially limiting fatty acid oxidation.
instruction: Discuss carnitine supplementation with your doctor if taking these antibiotics regularly for UTI prevention.
mechanism: Chronic administration depletes tissue carnitine through interaction with pivalate compounds

title: Valproic Acid ↔ Carnitine — Blood Level Reduction
detail: Valproic acid treatment reduces blood carnitine levels and may cause hepatotoxicity and increased plasma ammonia, leading to encephalopathy.
instruction: Monitor carnitine levels regularly. Intravenous L-carnitine may help treat valproic acid toxicity. Consult your doctor about supplementation.
mechanism: Valproic acid reduces blood carnitine levels and may cause hepatotoxicity with elevated ammonia

title: Phenobarbital ↔ Carnitine — Blood Level Reduction
detail: Treatment with phenobarbital anticonvulsant reduces blood levels of carnitine, potentially affecting carnitine-dependent metabolic functions.
instruction: Monitor carnitine levels if taking phenobarbital long-term. Discuss supplementation needs with your healthcare provider.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant treatment interferes with carnitine metabolism reducing blood levels

title: Phenytoin ↔ Carnitine — Blood Level Reduction
detail: Phenytoin anticonvulsant treatment reduces blood carnitine levels, which may impact fatty acid metabolism and energy production.
instruction: Have carnitine levels checked regularly if using phenytoin. Talk to your doctor about potential supplementation.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant therapy depletes blood carnitine through metabolic interference

title: Carbamazepine ↔ Carnitine — Blood Level Reduction
detail: Carbamazepine anticonvulsant treatment reduces blood carnitine concentrations, potentially affecting metabolic processes dependent on carnitine.
instruction: Monitor carnitine levels during carbamazepine therapy. Consult your healthcare provider about supplementation if needed.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant medication depletes blood carnitine levels

title: Anticonvulsants ↔ Carnitine — Depletion Risk
detail: Multiple anticonvulsant medications reduce blood carnitine levels. Valproic acid specifically may increase hepatotoxicity and ammonia levels when combined with other anticonvulsants.
instruction: Regular monitoring of carnitine levels is recommended for anyone on anticonvulsant therapy. Discuss supplementation with your doctor.
mechanism: Anticonvulsant drugs interfere with carnitine metabolism and blood levels

title: Sedatives ↔ Ashwagandha — enhanced sedation
detail: Ashwagandha may enhance sedative effects due to its sleep-promoting properties. The species name 'somnifera' means sleep-inducing, and clinical trials show improvements in sleep quality and latency.
instruction: Monitor for excessive sedation. Consult healthcare provider before combining with sleep medications or sedatives.
mechanism: Ashwagandha has inherent sleep-inducing properties that may additively enhance sedative drug effects
